questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: Percussive drilling breaks rock by
options: grinding the rock under continuous rotation only
repeated impact of a chisel/button bit, with intermittent rotation (indexing) between blows
melting the rock with a flame jet
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Percussion relies on impact energy.

**Reasoning.** A piston delivers repeated blows to the bit, which chips/crushes the rock; the bit is rotated a small amount (indexed) between blows so successive impacts strike fresh rock. Rotation itself does little cutting.

**Answer.** Repeated impact with intermittent indexing.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: In rotary drilling, rock is broken mainly by
options: crushing and shearing under high axial thrust combined with rotation of the bit
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Rotary drilling uses thrust and torque.

**Reasoning.** High pulldown (thrust) presses the tricone or drag bit into the rock while rotation makes the cutters crush/shear it; cuttings are removed by flushing. It is the dominant method for large-diameter opencast blastholes.

**Answer.** Crushing/shearing under thrust and rotation.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: A down-the-hole (DTH) hammer maintains penetration rate with depth because
options: the hammer is at the surface and energy travels down rods
the hammer is located just behind the bit at the hole bottom, so impact energy is not lost down long drill rods
it does not use compressed air
questions.solution:
**Concept.** DTH places the percussion mechanism at the bit.

**Reasoning.** Because the hammer strikes the bit directly at the bottom of the hole, energy transmission loss in the drill string is avoided, so penetration rate stays nearly constant even in deep holes — unlike top-hammer drilling.

**Answer.** Hammer at the hole bottom avoids rod energy loss.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: In top-hammer (out-of-hole) percussive drilling, penetration rate falls with depth because
options: impact energy is progressively lost in transmission through the lengthening drill rods/couplings
the rock becomes softer at depth
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Top-hammer energy must travel down the rod string.

**Reasoning.** Each rod joint and metre of steel dissipates part of the stress wave; the deeper the hole, the more energy is lost before reaching the bit, lowering penetration rate.

**Answer.** Energy lost down the lengthening drill string.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: Rotary-percussive drilling is best suited to
options: hard rock, combining impact (percussion) with rotation for efficient breakage
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Combining mechanisms suits hard ground.

**Reasoning.** Percussion chips the hard rock while rotation indexes and helps shear it; the combination gives high penetration in hard, competent formations where pure rotary would be slow.

**Answer.** Hard rock (impact + rotation).

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: A drill bores a 12 m hole in 4 minutes. The penetration rate, in m/min, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Penetration rate $=\dfrac{\text{hole depth}}{\text{drilling time}}$.

**Working.**
$$PR=\frac{12}{4}=3\ \text{m/min}$$

**Answer.** 3 m/min.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: Each 12 m hole takes 4 min to drill plus 1 min to reposition. In a 6 h (360 min) drilling shift, the number of holes completed is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Holes $=\dfrac{\text{available time}}{\text{time per hole}}$.

**Working.**
$$t_{hole}=4+1=5\ \text{min},\quad N=\frac{360}{5}=72$$

**Answer.** 72 holes.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: The drillability of a rock is most strongly governed by its
options: hardness/abrasiveness and strength (often correlated with quartz content)
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Drillability indexes how easily a rock is bored.

**Reasoning.** Hard, strong, abrasive rocks (high quartz) drill slowly and wear bits fast; soft, weak rocks drill quickly. Hence strength, hardness and abrasivity dominate.

**Answer.** Hardness/abrasiveness and strength.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: Drill-bit wear is greatest in
options: highly abrasive, high-quartz rock such as quartzite or sandstone
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Abrasion wears the cutting surfaces.

**Reasoning.** Hard quartz grains abrade button/insert bits rapidly, shortening bit life; soft, non-abrasive rocks cause little wear.

**Answer.** Abrasive high-quartz rock.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: A blast pattern has 50 holes, each 10 m deep. The total drilled meterage, in m, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Total metres $=$ holes $\times$ depth.

**Working.**
$$L=50\times10=500\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** 500 m.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: The purpose of flushing (with air, water or foam) during drilling is to
options: clear cuttings from the hole and cool/lubricate the bit
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Cuttings must be removed continuously.

**Reasoning.** The flushing medium lifts rock cuttings out of the hole, preventing regrinding and bit clogging, and cools the bit — all of which sustain penetration rate.

**Answer.** Remove cuttings and cool the bit.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: If drilling 500 m costs Rs 6000, the unit drilling cost, in Rs per metre, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Unit cost $=\dfrac{\text{total cost}}{\text{metres drilled}}$.

**Working.**
$$c=\frac{6000}{500}=12\ \text{Rs/m}$$

**Answer.** Rs 12 per metre.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: A tricone (roller) bit is used principally in
options: rotary drilling of medium-hard to hard rock
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Tricone bits crush rock with rolling cones.

**Reasoning.** Three toothed/insert cones rotate and crush the rock under thrust; they are the standard rotary bit for medium-hard to hard formations in opencast blasthole drilling.

**Answer.** Rotary drilling of medium-hard to hard rock.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: Drag (blade) bits are most suitable for
options: soft, non-abrasive formations such as coal or soft shale
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Drag bits shear soft rock with fixed blades.

**Reasoning.** Their cutting blades work well in soft, non-abrasive ground but wear out quickly in hard/abrasive rock, where they are uneconomic.

**Answer.** Soft, non-abrasive formations.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: A bit drills 2000 m before replacement. If each hole is 10 m deep, the number of holes drilled per bit is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Holes per bit $=\dfrac{\text{bit life (m)}}{\text{hole depth}}$.

**Working.**
$$N=\frac{2000}{10}=200$$

**Answer.** 200 holes.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: Large opencast benches generally use large-diameter blastholes (150–310 mm) because
options: they are cheaper to charge by hand
larger holes give higher drilling productivity and allow larger burdens/spacings and fewer holes per volume
they reduce the powder factor to zero
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Hole diameter scales the blast pattern.

**Reasoning.** A larger hole holds more explosive per metre, permitting larger burden and spacing, so fewer holes are needed to blast a given volume and drilling productivity per m³ rises — economic on big benches.

**Answer.** Higher productivity and larger patterns.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: A blasthole of 150 mm diameter is drilled to 12 m depth. The borehole volume, in m³, is ____ (three decimals).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Volume $=\dfrac{\pi}{4}d^2 L$.

**Working.**
$$V=\frac{\pi}{4}(0.15)^2(12)=0.7854\times0.0225\times12=0.212\ \text{m}^3$$

**Answer.** 0.212 m³.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: A 150 mm hole is charged with explosive of density 850 kg/m³ over a charge length of 8 m (fully coupled). The charge mass per hole, in kg, is ____ (one decimal).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Charge mass $=\dfrac{\pi}{4}d^2 L_c\,\rho$.

**Working.**
$$m=\frac{\pi}{4}(0.15)^2(8)(850)=0.01767\times8\times850=120.2\ \text{kg}$$

**Answer.** 120.2 kg.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: For a 150 mm hole fully coupled with 850 kg/m³ explosive, the linear charge concentration, in kg/m, is ____ (two decimals).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Linear concentration $=\dfrac{\pi}{4}d^2\rho$.

**Working.**
$$l=\frac{\pi}{4}(0.15)^2(850)=0.01767\times850=15.02\ \text{kg/m}$$

**Answer.** 15.02 kg/m.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: Decoupling the charge (using a charge diameter smaller than the hole diameter)
options: reduces the borehole/blast pressure on the wall, as used in smooth/controlled blasting
is only possible with detonating cord
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Decoupling leaves an air gap between charge and wall.

**Reasoning.** The annular gap cushions the detonation, lowering peak borehole pressure on the wall — this limits crushing/overbreak and is the basis of smooth-wall and presplit blasting.

**Answer.** Reduces borehole pressure (controlled blasting).

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: A 'fully coupled' charge is one in which
options: the charge diameter equals the borehole diameter (charge fills the hole)
two detonators are used per hole
the stemming length equals the charge length
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Coupling ratio $=$ charge dia / hole dia.

**Reasoning.** When this ratio is 1 the explosive fills the borehole cross-section (fully coupled), transmitting maximum pressure to the rock; ratios below 1 are decoupled.

**Answer.** Charge diameter equals hole diameter.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: Three holes of 10 m, 12 m and 14 m are drilled in a total of 12 min. The average penetration rate, in m/min, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Average rate $=\dfrac{\text{total metres}}{\text{total time}}$.

**Working.**
$$PR=\frac{10+12+14}{12}=\frac{36}{12}=3\ \text{m/min}$$

**Answer.** 3 m/min.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: A drill jumbo is used for
options: large-diameter rotary blasthole drilling in opencast benches
multi-boom hydraulic percussive drilling of the face in tunnels/drives
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Jumbos drill development rounds underground.

**Reasoning.** A jumbo carries two or more hydraulic drifters on booms to drill the many parallel holes of a tunnel/drift face quickly and accurately for blasting.

**Answer.** Multi-boom face drilling in tunnels/drives.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: A crawler (wagon) drill is
options: a fixed plant for crushing ore
a mobile, track-mounted percussive drill rig for surface blastholes
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Crawler drills are mobile surface rigs.

**Reasoning.** Mounted on crawler tracks, they tram around the bench and drill blastholes with a top-hammer or DTH; they are versatile for small to medium opencast and quarry work.

**Answer.** Mobile track-mounted surface blasthole drill.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: A face round needs 60 holes drilled in 30 min. If each jumbo boom drills one hole every 2 min, the number of booms required is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Holes per boom $=\dfrac{\text{time}}{\text{time per hole}}$; booms $=\dfrac{\text{total holes}}{\text{holes per boom}}$.

**Working.**
$$\text{holes/boom}=\frac{30}{2}=15,\quad N=\frac{60}{15}=4$$

**Answer.** 4 booms.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: In percussive drilling, the drill steel (rod) transmits
options: rotation and percussion (impact) energy from the drill to the bit
explosive to the bottom of the hole
questions.solution:
**Concept.** The rod couples the machine to the bit.

**Reasoning.** In top-hammer drilling the rod carries both the percussive stress wave and the rotation torque to the bit, and conveys flushing air through its bore. Energy losses along it limit hole depth.

**Answer.** Rotation and impact energy to the bit.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: In a soft rock the penetration rate is 4 m/min, while in a hard rock it is 2 m/min. The ratio of soft-to-hard penetration rate is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Ratio of the two rates.

**Working.**
$$\frac{4}{2}=2$$

**Answer.** 2 (soft rock drills twice as fast).

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: The 'specific drilling' (drill factor) of a blast is defined as
options: the metres drilled per cubic metre (or tonne) of rock blasted
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Specific drilling normalises drilling effort to broken rock.

**Reasoning.** It is the total drilled length divided by the volume (or tonnage) of rock the round breaks — an index of drilling efficiency that complements the powder factor.

**Answer.** Metres drilled per m³ (or tonne) blasted.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: ANFO, the most widely used bulk blasting agent, is a mixture of
questions.solution:
**Concept.** ANFO = Ammonium Nitrate + Fuel Oil.

**Reasoning.** Porous prilled ammonium nitrate (~94%) absorbs fuel oil (~6%) to give a near oxygen-balanced, cheap, dry-hole blasting agent that is detonator-insensitive (needs a booster).

**Answer.** ~94% AN with ~6% fuel oil.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: In an oxygen-balanced ANFO of 94% ammonium nitrate and the rest fuel oil, the fuel-oil content, in % by weight, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** The two fractions sum to 100%.

**Working.**
$$\text{FO}=100-94=6\%$$

**Answer.** 6%.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: ANFO is unsuitable for wet (water-filled) blastholes because
options: ammonium nitrate is water-soluble and the mixture is desensitised by water
it cannot be loaded by hand
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Water dissolves and desensitises ANFO.

**Reasoning.** Water leaches the soluble AN and separates the fuel oil, so the charge fails to detonate reliably; wet holes need water-resistant slurries/emulsions.

**Answer.** AN is water-soluble and water desensitises it.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: An emulsion explosive is essentially
options: a water-in-oil emulsion of fine oxidiser droplets, giving excellent water resistance
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Emulsions are oxidiser droplets dispersed in oil.

**Reasoning.** Microscopic ammonium-nitrate solution droplets coated by an oil/fuel phase give intimate oxidiser-fuel contact, high density and very good water resistance — ideal for wet holes; sensitised by gas bubbles or microballoons.

**Answer.** Water-in-oil emulsion (water resistant).

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: A slurry (watergel) explosive is
options: a dry powder with no water
a thickened/gelled aqueous solution of ammonium nitrate (often with sensitisers), water resistant
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Slurries are gelled aqueous AN.

**Reasoning.** A cross-linked gel holds the AN solution and sensitisers; the gel resists water, so slurries (watergels) suit wet holes, though emulsions have now largely superseded them.

**Answer.** Gelled aqueous AN solution (water resistant).

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: The velocity of detonation (VOD) of an explosive is
options: the speed at which the detonation wave travels along the explosive column
the speed of the drill bit
the rate at which safety fuse burns
questions.solution:
**Concept.** VOD characterises detonation propagation.

**Reasoning.** It is the velocity of the reaction front along the charge (typically 2000–7000 m/s); higher VOD gives higher detonation pressure and shattering (brisant) action.

**Answer.** Speed of the detonation wave along the column.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: A 6 m explosive column has a VOD of 4000 m/s. The time for the detonation to traverse the full column, in milliseconds, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $t=\dfrac{L}{\text{VOD}}$.

**Working.**
$$t=\frac{6}{4000}=1.5\times10^{-3}\ \text{s}=1.5\ \text{ms}$$

**Answer.** 1.5 ms.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: Using $P_d=\rho D^2/4$, an explosive of density 1200 kg/m³ and VOD 5000 m/s has a detonation pressure, in GPa, of ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Detonation pressure $P_d=\dfrac{\rho D^2}{4}$.

**Working.**
$$P_d=\frac{1200\times(5000)^2}{4}=\frac{1200\times25\times10^6}{4}=7.5\times10^9\ \text{Pa}=7.5\ \text{GPa}$$

**Answer.** 7.5 GPa.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: An explosive with zero oxygen balance is desirable because the detonation products are
options: mainly carbon monoxide and nitric oxides
mainly the least-toxic CO₂, H₂O and N₂
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Oxygen balance measures excess/deficit of oxygen.

**Reasoning.** At zero OB the fuel and oxidiser react completely to CO₂, H₂O and N₂, minimising toxic CO and NOx fumes — important underground.

**Answer.** Least-toxic CO₂, H₂O, N₂.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: A negative oxygen balance (fuel excess) tends to produce
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Fuel-rich detonation lacks oxygen for full oxidation.

**Reasoning.** With too little oxygen, carbon is only partly oxidised, giving toxic CO; conversely a positive (oxygen-excess) balance gives brown NOx fumes. Balanced charges minimise both.

**Answer.** Carbon monoxide (CO).

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: Permitted (permissible) explosives are required in gassy coal mines because they
options: have a low flame temperature and short flame duration, reducing the risk of igniting firedamp/coal dust
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Permitted explosives are designed against gas ignition.

**Reasoning.** Flame inhibitors (e.g. salts) lower flame temperature and shorten the flame, so the detonation is far less likely to ignite methane (firedamp) or coal dust in gassy mines.

**Answer.** Low/short flame to avoid igniting firedamp.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: The function of a detonator (blasting cap) is to
options: provide the initial shock/energy that sets off the main explosive charge or booster
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Detonators initiate the explosive train.

**Reasoning.** A small primary charge in the cap produces a sharp shock that detonates the cap-sensitive primer/booster, which in turn fires the insensitive bulk explosive.

**Answer.** Provide the initiating shock.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: Delay detonators are used in a blast round to
options: make all holes fire at exactly the same instant
fire holes in a timed sequence, improving fragmentation and reducing ground vibration
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Sequenced firing creates relief and reduces charge per delay.

**Reasoning.** Each delay lets earlier holes create a free face for later holes (better fragmentation/throw) and reduces the maximum instantaneous charge, lowering peak ground vibration.

**Answer.** Timed firing for fragmentation and lower vibration.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: A blast has 8 rows fired with a uniform 25 ms delay between consecutive rows. The total firing time spread from first to last row, in ms, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Spread $=(\text{rows}-1)\times\text{delay}$.

**Working.**
$$\Delta t=(8-1)\times25=175\ \text{ms}$$

**Answer.** 175 ms.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: Detonating cord (e.g. Cordtex) is
options: a slow-burning fuse used to fire a plain detonator
a flexible cord with a high-VOD PETN core used to initiate charges along the trunkline/down the hole
an inert tube carrying flushing air
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Detonating cord detonates (it does not burn slowly).

**Reasoning.** Its PETN core detonates at ~6500–7000 m/s, so it reliably initiates boosters/charges and links holes on the surface trunkline; it is fired by a detonator.

**Answer.** High-VOD PETN-cored initiating cord.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: Safety fuse is used to
options: detonate at high velocity along the trunkline
burn at a controlled rate and fire a plain detonator after a set time delay
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Safety fuse burns (deflagrates) at a fixed rate.

**Reasoning.** Its blackpowder core burns at a standard rate (about 120 s/m), giving the shotfirer time to retreat before the spit reaches and fires a plain detonator.

**Answer.** Controlled burning to delay-fire a plain detonator.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: Safety fuse burns at 120 s per metre. The burning time for a 1.5 m length, in seconds, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Time $=$ rate $\times$ length.

**Working.**
$$t=120\times1.5=180\ \text{s}$$

**Answer.** 180 s (3 minutes).

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: A primer (booster) is used in a blasthole to
options: stem the collar of the hole
provide a cap-sensitive high-explosive charge that reliably initiates insensitive bulk explosives such as ANFO or emulsion
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Bulk explosives need a booster to detonate.

**Reasoning.** ANFO and emulsions are not cap-sensitive; a cast booster or primer cartridge (fired by a detonator/detcord) supplies the high-energy shock needed to start full detonation of the column.

**Answer.** Cap-sensitive charge that initiates the bulk explosive.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: A key advantage of the Nonel (shock-tube) initiation system over electric detonators is that it is
options: immune to stray electric currents, static and radio-frequency hazards
able to work without any detonator
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Shock tube carries a low-energy signal, not electricity.

**Reasoning.** A thin reactive dust layer inside the plastic tube propagates a shock wave to the detonator; since no electric current is involved, it is unaffected by stray currents, static or RF — a major safety benefit.

**Answer.** Immune to stray/electric hazards.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: Electronic detonators differ from pyrotechnic-delay detonators chiefly in offering
options: highly accurate, programmable delay times with negligible scatter
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Electronic delays use a microchip timer.

**Reasoning.** Instead of a burning pyrotechnic element (which scatters), a programmable chip gives precise, freely chosen delays with millisecond accuracy, allowing optimised timing for fragmentation and vibration control.

**Answer.** Accurate, programmable delays.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: A cylindrical cartridge of 32 mm diameter and 200 mm length has an explosive density of 1200 kg/m³. Its mass, in grams, is ____ (nearest gram).

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: A blasthole requires a 10 kg charge made up of 0.25 kg cartridges. The number of cartridges needed is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Cartridges $=\dfrac{\text{charge mass}}{\text{cartridge mass}}$.

**Working.**
$$N=\frac{10}{0.25}=40$$

**Answer.** 40 cartridges.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: Among ANFO, watergel slurry and emulsion, the best water resistance is offered by
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Water resistance depends on the physical form.

**Reasoning.** ANFO has essentially no water resistance; slurries are good; the oil-continuous phase of an emulsion seals the oxidiser droplets, giving excellent water resistance — the best of the three.

**Answer.** Emulsion.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: The 'fume class' of an explosive indicates
options: the quantity of toxic gases (CO, NOx) produced on detonation — important for underground use
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Fume class rates noxious gas yield.

**Reasoning.** It classifies explosives by the volume of poisonous fumes per unit mass; low-fume (class 1) explosives are preferred underground where ventilation must clear the gases.

**Answer.** Toxic-gas yield on detonation.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: In a continuous VOD test the detonation front travels 1 m between two probes in 0.2 ms. The measured VOD, in m/s, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $\text{VOD}=\dfrac{\text{distance}}{\text{time}}$.

**Working.**
$$\text{VOD}=\frac{1}{0.2\times10^{-3}}=5000\ \text{m/s}$$

**Answer.** 5000 m/s.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: An explosive described as 'cap-sensitive' is one that
options: can be reliably initiated by a standard No. 8 blasting detonator without a booster
requires a 100 g booster to fire
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Cap sensitivity is the ease of initiation.

**Reasoning.** A cap-sensitive explosive (e.g. many cartridged NG/emulsion products) detonates directly from a No. 8 cap; insensitive blasting agents (ANFO) are not cap-sensitive and need a booster.

**Answer.** Initiated directly by a No. 8 detonator.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: A major advantage of pumped/augered bulk explosives (ANFO, bulk emulsion) over cartridged explosives is that they
options: completely fill the borehole cross-section (good coupling), load quickly and cost less per kg
cannot be used in large holes
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Bulk loading fully couples the charge.

**Reasoning.** Bulk products fill the whole hole diameter, maximising coupling and energy transfer, are loaded fast by truck, and are cheaper than packaged cartridges — hence dominant in large opencast operations.

**Answer.** Full coupling, fast loading, lower cost.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: Explosive A has a weight strength of 1000 and explosive B has 900 (same units). The relative weight strength of B with respect to A, in %, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Relative weight strength $=\dfrac{\text{strength of B}}{\text{strength of A}}\times100$.

**Working.**
$$RWS=\frac{900}{1000}\times100=90\%$$

**Answer.** 90%.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: In bench blasting, the burden is
options: the distance between two holes in the same row
the perpendicular distance from a blasthole to the nearest free face
the length of stemming in the collar
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Burden is measured toward the free face.

**Reasoning.** It is the shortest (perpendicular) distance from the charge to the nearest free face, i.e. the rock the hole must break forward; it is the most critical blast-design dimension.

**Answer.** Perpendicular distance to the nearest free face.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: The spacing in a blast pattern is
options: the distance between adjacent holes measured along a row
the distance from the hole to the free face
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Spacing is the in-row hole separation.

**Reasoning.** It is the distance between neighbouring holes in the same row (perpendicular to the burden direction); together with the burden it sets the volume each hole breaks.

**Answer.** Distance between adjacent holes in a row.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: Using a rule of thumb of burden = 30 × hole diameter, the burden for a 150 mm hole, in m, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $B=30\,d$ (a common opencast guideline).

**Working.**
$$B=30\times0.15=4.5\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** 4.5 m.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: For a staggered pattern with spacing = 1.25 × burden and a burden of 4.5 m, the spacing, in m, is ____ (two decimals).

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: The function of stemming (inert material placed in the collar of the hole) is to
options: increase the VOD of the explosive
confine the detonation gases so they do their work on the rock, reducing airblast and flyrock
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Stemming confines the borehole.

**Reasoning.** Drill cuttings/angular gravel in the collar resist the gas pressure long enough for the explosive energy to fragment and heave the rock instead of venting up the hole — cutting airblast and flyrock.

**Answer.** Confine gases; reduce airblast/flyrock.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: Taking stemming length = 0.7 × burden, the stemming for a 4.5 m burden, in m, is ____ (two decimals).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $T=0.7\,B$.

**Working.**
$$T=0.7\times4.5=3.15\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** 3.15 m.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: Sub-grade (sub-drilling) drilling below the bench floor is provided to
options: ensure the charge breaks the rock down to floor level and avoids a hard toe/high bottom
questions.solution:
**Concept.** The charge is least effective near its bottom.

**Reasoning.** Drilling a little below grade places explosive energy at floor level so the rock shears to the design floor, preventing toe (unbroken bottom) that would hamper loading and the next bench.

**Answer.** Break to floor level / avoid a hard toe.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: With sub-drill = 0.3 × burden, the sub-drilling depth for a 4.5 m burden, in m, is ____ (two decimals).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $J=0.3\,B$.

**Working.**
$$J=0.3\times4.5=1.35\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** 1.35 m.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: For a 10 m bench with 1.35 m of sub-drilling, the total blasthole depth, in m, is ____ (two decimals).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Hole depth $=$ bench height $+$ sub-drill.

**Working.**
$$H_{hole}=10+1.35=11.35\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** 11.35 m.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: A blasthole is 11.35 m deep with 3.15 m of stemming. The explosive column (charge) length, in m, is ____ (two decimals).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Charge length $=$ hole depth $-$ stemming.

**Working.**
$$L_c=11.35-3.15=8.20\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** 8.2 m.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: A charge column of 8.2 m has a linear charge concentration of 15 kg/m. The explosive mass per hole, in kg, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Charge mass $=$ column length $\times$ linear concentration.

**Working.**
$$Q=8.2\times15=123\ \text{kg}$$

**Answer.** 123 kg.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: The powder factor (specific charge) of a blast is
options: the number of holes per row
the mass of explosive used per unit volume (or tonne) of rock broken
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Powder factor normalises explosive to broken rock.

**Reasoning.** Expressed as kg/m³ or kg/tonne, it measures how much explosive is consumed per unit of rock blasted — a key index of blast efficiency and cost.

**Answer.** Explosive mass per m³ (or tonne) of rock.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: A blasthole holds 120 kg of explosive and breaks 250 m³ of rock. The powder factor, in kg/m³, is ____ (two decimals).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $PF=\dfrac{\text{explosive mass}}{\text{volume broken}}$.

**Working.**
$$PF=\frac{120}{250}=0.48\ \text{kg/m}^3$$

**Answer.** 0.48 kg/m³.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: If the powder factor is 0.48 kg/m³ and the rock density is 2.5 t/m³, the powder factor, in kg/tonne, is ____ (three decimals).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $PF_{mass}=\dfrac{PF_{vol}}{\rho}$.

**Working.**
$$PF=\frac{0.48}{2.5}=0.192\ \text{kg/t}$$

**Answer.** 0.192 kg/tonne.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: A round has 40 holes, each charged with 120 kg of explosive. The total explosive used, in kg, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Total $=$ holes $\times$ charge per hole.

**Working.**
$$Q_{tot}=40\times120=4800\ \text{kg}$$

**Answer.** 4800 kg.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: If each of the 40 holes breaks 250 m³, the total rock volume broken by the round, in m³, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Total volume $=$ holes $\times$ volume per hole.

**Working.**
$$V=40\times250=10000\ \text{m}^3$$

**Answer.** 10000 m³.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: For 10000 m³ of rock of density 2.5 t/m³, the tonnage broken, in tonnes, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Tonnage $=$ volume $\times$ density.

**Working.**
$$T=10000\times2.5=25000\ \text{t}$$

**Answer.** 25000 t.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: For the bench blast geometry shown, with burden 4.5 m and spacing 5.6 m, the plan area broken per hole (B × S), in m², is ____ (one decimal).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Each hole is responsible for a plan rectangle of burden × spacing.

**Working.**
$$A=B\times S=4.5\times5.6=25.2\ \text{m}^2$$

**Answer.** 25.2 m².

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: In good blast-pattern design the spacing-to-burden ratio (S/B) is usually kept around
questions.solution:
**Concept.** S/B controls fragmentation uniformity.

**Reasoning.** Field practice and design rules (e.g. Konya) keep S/B between about 1.1 and 1.5; too low wastes holes, too high leaves coarse rock between holes.

**Answer.** About 1.1–1.5.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: If the burden is much larger than the design value, the likely result is
options: poor fragmentation, toe and back-break with possible cratering of the collar
complete loss of all explosive energy as heat only
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Burden sets how much rock each charge must move.

**Reasoning.** Excess burden over-confines the charge: the rock breaks coarsely, leaves toe and back-break, and gases vent up the collar. (Too small a burden causes the opposite — flyrock and airblast.)

**Answer.** Poor fragmentation, toe and back-break.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: A fixed 4800 kg of explosive is used. If the powder factor is raised from 0.40 to 0.50 kg/m³, the rock volume that can be broken at 0.50 kg/m³, in m³, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $V=\dfrac{Q}{PF}$ for a fixed explosive mass.

**Working.**
$$V=\frac{4800}{0.50}=9600\ \text{m}^3$$

**Answer.** 9600 m³.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: The Kuz-Ram model is used to predict
options: the ground vibration peak particle velocity
the fragment-size distribution of a blast from rock and blast-design parameters
the cost of drilling per metre
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Kuz-Ram combines Kuznetsov mean size with Rosin-Rammler distribution.

**Reasoning.** It estimates the mean fragment size from powder factor, rock factor and explosive energy, then describes the size distribution about that mean — a standard fragmentation-prediction tool.

**Answer.** Blast fragment-size distribution.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: A blasthole must break 240 m³ of rock at a powder factor of 0.5 kg/m³. The required charge per hole, in kg, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $Q=PF\times V$.

**Working.**
$$Q=0.5\times240=120\ \text{kg}$$

**Answer.** 120 kg.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: A 'toe' in bench blasting refers to
options: unbroken rock left standing at the bench floor when sub-drilling or burden is inadequate
the stemming column at the collar
the free face of the bench
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Toe is bottom rock that fails to break.

**Reasoning.** Insufficient bottom charge (too little sub-drill or too large a burden) leaves a ridge of hard rock at floor level — the toe — which obstructs loading and raises the floor.

**Answer.** Unbroken rock at the bench floor.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: A round uses 4800 kg of explosive at Rs 50/kg to break 25000 t of rock. The explosive cost, in Rs per tonne, is ____ (one decimal).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Unit cost $=\dfrac{\text{total explosive cost}}{\text{tonnes broken}}$.

**Working.**
$$\text{Cost}=4800\times50=\text{Rs }240000,\quad \frac{240000}{25000}=9.6\ \text{Rs/t}$$

**Answer.** Rs 9.6 per tonne.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: Deck charging (decking) of a blasthole means
options: filling the whole hole with one continuous charge
splitting the charge into separate decks separated by inert stemming, often fired on different delays
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Decking distributes explosive along the hole.

**Reasoning.** Inert stemming separates the column into decks (sometimes on different delays); this distributes energy, can avoid weak/waste bands, and reduces the charge weight per delay for vibration control.

**Answer.** Charge split into decks by stemming.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: A round of 40 holes is laid out with 8 holes per row. The number of rows is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Rows $=\dfrac{\text{total holes}}{\text{holes per row}}$.

**Working.**
$$N=\frac{40}{8}=5$$

**Answer.** 5 rows.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: Which of the following generally IMPROVE blast fragmentation? (Select all that apply.)
options: Using appropriate inter-hole and inter-row delays to create free faces
Maintaining an adequate powder factor for the rock type
Choosing a spacing-to-burden ratio in the range 1.1–1.5
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Fragmentation depends on relief, energy and geometry.

**Reasoning.** Correct delays provide free faces for breakage; an adequate powder factor supplies enough energy; a sound S/B ratio (1.1–1.5) gives uniform breakage. An excessive burden over-confines the charge and worsens fragmentation, so it is not beneficial.

**Answer.** Options 1, 2 and 3.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: In presplit blasting, the presplit holes are
options: heavily charged and fired after the main production holes
closely spaced, lightly/decoupled charged and fired before the main blast to form a clean fracture plane along the final wall
left completely empty and never fired
fired with the maximum charge per delay
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Presplitting creates the final wall fracture first.

**Reasoning.** A row of closely spaced, lightly (decoupled) charged holes is detonated ahead of the main blast; the cracks link between holes to form a continuous plane that protects the final wall from production-blast damage.

**Answer.** Light decoupled charges fired first along the wall.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: Smooth-wall (cushion/trim) blasting differs from presplitting in that the perimeter holes are
options: fired first, before any production holes
fired last in the round, with light decoupled charges, to trim the wall after the bulk is removed
charged more heavily than production holes
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Smooth blasting trims the wall at the end.

**Reasoning.** The lightly charged, decoupled perimeter holes are detonated last (after the buffer/production rows), shaving a smooth final face; presplitting instead fires the perimeter first.

**Answer.** Fired last with light decoupled charges.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: Using a presplit hole spacing of 10 × hole diameter, the spacing for 100 mm presplit holes, in m, is ____ (one decimal).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Presplit spacing is much smaller than production spacing.

**Working.**
$$S=10\times0.10=1.0\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** 1.0 m.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: Line drilling as a controlled-blasting technique uses
options: a row of closely spaced unloaded (empty) holes that form a plane of weakness guiding the break
detonating cord laid on the surface
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Line drilling provides a guide plane without explosive.

**Reasoning.** Many closely spaced empty holes create a weakened surface along the final line; the production blast then tends to break to this plane, limiting overbreak. It is often combined with presplit/smooth blasting.

**Answer.** Closely spaced empty holes forming a weakness plane.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: Blast-induced ground vibration is most commonly quantified by the
questions.solution:
**Concept.** PPV correlates with structural damage potential.

**Reasoning.** The peak particle velocity of the ground motion (mm/s) is the standard damage criterion in blasting regulations; limits are set by frequency and structure type.

**Answer.** Peak particle velocity (PPV).

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: For a distance of 100 m and a maximum charge per delay of 50 kg, the square-root scaled distance $SD=D/\sqrt{W}$, in m/kg$^{0.5}$, is ____ (two decimals).

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: Using $PPV=K\,(SD)^{-n}$ with $K=1140$, $n=1.6$ and $SD=14.14$ m/kg$^{0.5}$, the predicted PPV, in mm/s, is ____ (one decimal).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Empirical attenuation law $PPV=K\,SD^{-n}$.

**Working.**
$$SD^{1.6}=14.14^{1.6}=69.3$$
$$PPV=\frac{1140}{69.3}=16.45\ \text{mm/s}$$

**Answer.** 16.45 mm/s.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: The maximum charge fired per delay is deliberately limited primarily to
options: keep ground vibration (PPV) at nearby structures within safe limits
questions.solution:
**Concept.** PPV scales with charge per delay, not the total round.

**Reasoning.** Since scaled distance uses the maximum instantaneous charge, sequencing the round with delays so that little explosive fires at once keeps vibration below the damage threshold for surrounding structures.

**Answer.** Control ground vibration within safe limits.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: To keep the scaled distance at least 20 m/kg$^{0.5}$ at a structure 100 m away, the maximum charge per delay, in kg, must not exceed ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** From $SD=D/\sqrt{W}$, $W=(D/SD)^2$.

**Working.**
$$W_{max}=\left(\frac{100}{20}\right)^2=5^2=25\ \text{kg}$$

**Answer.** 25 kg.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: Air-overpressure (airblast) from a blast is aggravated by
options: inadequate stemming, exposed detonating cord on the surface and too small a burden
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Airblast is a pressure pulse from venting gases.

**Reasoning.** Poor confinement — short stemming, surface detcord, small burden — lets gases escape to the atmosphere abruptly, generating air-overpressure (and noise). Good confinement minimises it.

**Answer.** Inadequate stemming/exposed detcord/small burden.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: Flyrock (rock thrown beyond the intended blast area) is most often caused by
options: too large a burden and too much stemming
insufficient burden/stemming or geological weakness allowing gas to vent and eject rock
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Flyrock results from uncontrolled gas escape.

**Reasoning.** When the burden or stemming is too small, or a weak seam/cavity exists, high-pressure gases blow out and hurl fragments far beyond the muckpile — the leading cause of blasting injuries.

**Answer.** Insufficient burden/stemming or geological weakness.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: If 5 holes, each charged with 120 kg, are all fired on the same delay number, the charge per delay, in kg, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Charge per delay $=$ holes on that delay $\times$ charge per hole.

**Working.**
$$W=5\times120=600\ \text{kg}$$

**Answer.** 600 kg.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: Secondary blasting is carried out to
options: break oversize boulders/toe left after the primary blast, e.g. by blockholing or mudcapping
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Secondary blasting handles oversize.

**Reasoning.** Boulders too large for the crusher/loader are broken by drilling and charging them (blockholing) or by placing a charge on the surface under a mud cover (mudcapping/plaster shooting).

**Answer.** Break oversize boulders after the primary blast.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: In plaster (mudcap) secondary blasting, the charge is
options: placed inside a drilled hole in the boulder
laid on the boulder surface and covered with stiff clay/mud — no drilling required
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Mudcapping needs no drilling.

**Reasoning.** A charge is placed on the boulder and confined by a mud cap; the detonation breaks the boulder by surface shock. It saves drilling but uses more explosive than blockholing.

**Answer.** Charge on the surface under a mud cover.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: In an underground tunnel/drift blast round, the 'cut' holes are
options: the group of holes fired first to open an initial cavity/free face for the rest of the round
questions.solution:
**Concept.** The cut creates the second free face.

**Reasoning.** A tunnel face has only one free face; the cut holes (burn or angled) fire first to blast out a small opening, giving the easer/perimeter holes a free face to break toward.

**Answer.** Holes fired first to create an initial opening.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: A burn (parallel-hole) cut achieves a deep pull in small headings because
options: all holes are angled steeply into the face
parallel holes are drilled with one or more large-diameter uncharged relief holes, into which the charged cut holes break, forming a deep cavity
it uses no explosive at all
it is fired before the holes are drilled
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Burn cuts use uncharged relief holes parallel to the bore axis.

**Reasoning.** The empty large-diameter hole(s) give the surrounding charged holes a free face to break into; because the holes are parallel (not angled) the round can be drilled to the full advance even in a narrow heading, giving a deep pull.

**Answer.** Parallel holes break into empty relief holes.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: A wedge (V) cut consists of
options: parallel holes with an empty relief hole
pairs of holes drilled at an angle to meet, so a wedge-shaped prism of rock is blasted out to form the cut
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Angled-hole cuts wedge out a prism.

**Reasoning.** Opposing inclined holes meet near the back of the cut; firing them ejects a V-shaped wedge, opening the face. It needs a reasonably wide heading because of the angled drilling, and the pull is limited by face width.

**Answer.** Angled holes blasting out a wedge prism.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: Holes are drilled to 3.0 m in a heading and the round achieves 90% pull. The advance per round, in m, is ____ (one decimal).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Advance $=$ hole depth $\times$ pull efficiency.

**Working.**
$$\text{Advance}=3.0\times0.90=2.7\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** 2.7 m.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: A 12 m² heading advances 2.7 m per round at a specific charge of 1.5 kg/m³. The explosive consumed per round, in kg, is ____ (one decimal).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Explosive $=$ volume blasted $\times$ specific charge.

**Working.**
$$V=12\times2.7=32.4\ \text{m}^3$$
$$Q=32.4\times1.5=48.6\ \text{kg}$$

**Answer.** 48.6 kg.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: If one blasting cycle takes 2 h and advances 2.7 m, the heading advance in an 8 h shift, in m, is ____ (one decimal).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Rounds $=$ shift / cycle time; advance $=$ rounds $\times$ pull.

**Working.**
$$N=\frac{8}{2}=4,\quad \text{advance}=4\times2.7=10.8\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** 10.8 m.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: A 12 m² face is drilled at a density of 2.5 holes per m². The number of holes in the round is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Holes $=$ face area $\times$ hole density.

**Working.**
$$N=12\times2.5=30$$

**Answer.** 30 holes.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: In a burn cut, the uncharged large-diameter relief hole serves to
options: provide a void/free face into which the charged cut holes can break, improving pull
questions.solution:
**Concept.** The relief hole supplies the only free face for the cut.

**Reasoning.** With no free face except the heading itself, the empty relief hole gives the adjacent charged holes somewhere to throw rock; without it the parallel cut would 'freeze' and the round would fail to pull.

**Answer.** Free face/void for the cut holes to break into.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: A round of 30 holes each 3.0 m deep blasts 32.4 m³ of rock. The specific drilling, in m per m³, is ____ (two decimals).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Specific drilling $=\dfrac{\text{total drilled length}}{\text{volume blasted}}$.

**Working.**
$$L=30\times3.0=90\ \text{m},\quad \frac{90}{32.4}=2.78\ \text{m/m}^3$$

**Answer.** 2.78 m/m³.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: A misfire (charge that fails to detonate) must be dealt with by
options: immediately re-drilling into the same hole
following statutory procedure — wait the prescribed time, do not drill into the charge, and dispose of it safely under supervision
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Misfires are a major hazard governed by regulation.

**Reasoning.** Statutory rules require a waiting period before re-approach, prohibit drilling into a charged hole, and specify safe disposal (e.g. washing out or blasting an adjacent relief hole) under the blaster's supervision.

**Answer.** Follow statutory misfire procedure.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: Sympathetic detonation refers to
options: an explosive burning slowly instead of detonating
the detonation of one charge being initiated by the shock of a neighbouring charge across the intervening ground
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Shock can jump between nearby charges.

**Reasoning.** If holes are too close, the detonation of one can set off an adjacent charge through the ground (sympathetic detonation), upsetting the intended delay sequence and raising the charge per delay — hence minimum hole-spacing rules.

**Answer.** One charge initiating an adjacent charge via shock.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: Which measures help reduce blast-induced ground vibration at nearby structures? (Select all that apply.)
options: Reducing the maximum charge fired per delay
Using delay detonators instead of instantaneous firing
Firing all holes simultaneously on a single delay
Increasing the distance between the blast and the structure
questions.solution:
**Concept.** PPV falls with smaller charge per delay and larger scaled distance.

**Reasoning.** Limiting the charge per delay and sequencing with delays both lower the instantaneous charge, while greater distance increases the scaled distance — all reduce PPV. Firing everything on one delay maximises the instantaneous charge and worsens vibration.

**Answer.** Options 1, 2 and 4.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: A bench round (see figure) has 5 holes, each with burden $B=3$ m, spacing $S=4$ m and bench height $H=10$ m, charged with 40 kg of explosive per hole. The powder factor, in $\mathrm{kg/m^3}$, is _____ (two decimals).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Powder factor $=\dfrac{\text{total explosive}}{\text{rock volume}}$.

**Working.**
$$V=B\,S\,H\times n=3\times4\times10\times5=600\ \mathrm{m^3}$$
$$W=40\times5=200\ \text{kg},\quad PF=\frac{200}{600}=0.33\ \mathrm{kg/m^3}$$

**Answer.** $0.33\ \mathrm{kg/m^3}$.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: In the tunnel face drill round shown, the small cluster of holes at the centre (red/black) is fired first. These central holes constitute the

figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 200 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='20' y='20' width='160' height='160' fill='#e5e7eb' stroke='#475569' stroke-width='2'/><g fill='#1f2937'><circle cx='90' cy='100' r='4'/><circle cx='110' cy='100' r='4'/><circle cx='100' cy='88' r='4'/><circle cx='100' cy='112' r='4'/></g><circle cx='100' cy='100' r='4' fill='#dc2626'/><g fill='#2563eb'><circle cx='50' cy='50' r='4'/><circle cx='150' cy='50' r='4'/><circle cx='50' cy='150' r='4'/><circle cx='150' cy='150' r='4'/><circle cx='100' cy='40' r='4'/><circle cx='100' cy='160' r='4'/><circle cx='40' cy='100' r='4'/><circle cx='160' cy='100' r='4'/></g><text x='100' y='195' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>tunnel face round</text></svg>
figure.caption: Holes in a tunnel face round

options: cut holes, which create the initial free face for the rest of the round
questions.solution:
**Concept.** The cut opens a second free face.

**Reasoning.** In a confined tunnel face the cut holes (burn or wedge/V cut) are blasted first to create an opening; the easers, then the perimeter and lifters break toward this new free face.

**Answer.** Cut holes.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: A bench blasthole (see figure) is 11 m deep and is collared with 3 m of stemming. The length of the explosive charge column, in m, is _____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Charge column $=$ hole depth $-$ stemming length.

**Working.**
$$L_c=11-3=8\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** $8\ \text{m}$.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: A structure stands 100 m from a blast (see figure) in which the maximum charge per delay is 25 kg. The square-root scaled distance $\left(D/\sqrt{W}\right)$, in $\mathrm{m/kg^{0.5}}$, is _____.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 280 140' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><circle cx='40' cy='100' r='8' fill='#dc2626'/><text x='40' y='124' font-size='9' text-anchor='middle'>blast</text><path d='M210 100 L210 70 L230 55 L250 70 L250 100 Z' fill='#fde68a' stroke='#b45309'/><text x='230' y='124' font-size='9' text-anchor='middle'>structure</text><line x1='50' y1='100' x2='205' y2='100' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='1' marker-end='url(#dde)'/><defs><marker id='dde' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0 0 L6 3 L0 6 Z' fill='#2563eb'/></marker></defs><text x='125' y='94' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle' fill='#2563eb'>D = 100 m</text></svg>
figure.caption: Blast and nearby structure

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Scaled distance $SD=\dfrac{D}{\sqrt{W}}$.

**Working.**
$$SD=\frac{100}{\sqrt{25}}=\frac{100}{5}=20\ \mathrm{m/kg^{0.5}}$$

**Answer.** $20\ \mathrm{m/kg^{0.5}}$.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: In the figure, line A is a row of closely spaced, lightly charged holes drilled along the final excavation limit and fired before the main (production) holes. This is the technique of

figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='30' y1='20' x2='30' y2='135' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-width='1' stroke-dasharray='3 3'/><g fill='#1f2937'><circle cx='30' cy='35' r='3'/><circle cx='30' cy='55' r='3'/><circle cx='30' cy='75' r='3'/><circle cx='30' cy='95' r='3'/><circle cx='30' cy='115' r='3'/></g><g fill='#dc2626'><circle cx='90' cy='45' r='4'/><circle cx='90' cy='95' r='4'/><circle cx='150' cy='45' r='4'/><circle cx='150' cy='95' r='4'/></g><text x='30' y='152' font-size='9' text-anchor='middle'>line A</text><text x='120' y='152' font-size='9' text-anchor='middle'>production holes</text></svg>
figure.caption: Final-wall holes (A) and production holes

options: presplit blasting, which forms a clean fracture plane along the final wall and reduces overbreak
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Presplitting protects the final wall.

**Reasoning.** Closely spaced, lightly charged holes fired first create a continuous crack along the design line; the main blast then breaks toward it, leaving a smooth, stable face with less overbreak and back-damage.

**Answer.** Presplit blasting.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: For the bench pattern shown (burden 3 m, spacing 4 m, bench height 10 m) with 30 kg of explosive per hole, compute the powder factor (kg/m³).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Powder factor = charge per hole ÷ rock volume per hole (B × S × H).

**Working.**
$$PF = \frac{30}{3 \times 4 \times 10} = \frac{30}{120} = 0.25$$

**Answer.** $\mathbf{0.25}$ kg/m³.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: Using the rule-of-thumb $B = K_b \, d$ with burden ratio $K_b = 30$ and hole diameter $d = 0.1$ m (bench as shown), compute the burden (m).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Burden scales with hole diameter via the burden ratio.

**Working.**
$$B = K_b \, d = 30 \times 0.1 = 3$$

**Answer.** $\mathbf{3}$ m.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: For the bench shown with a burden of 3 m, compute the stemming length (m) using the common rule stemming = 0.7 × burden.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Stemming length is taken as about 0.7 of the burden.

**Working.**
$$T = 0.7 \times 3 = 2.1$$

**Answer.** $\mathbf{2.1}$ m.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: For the bench shown with a burden of 3 m, compute the subdrill depth (m) using subdrill = 0.3 × burden.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Subdrilling below grade is about 0.3 of the burden to ensure a clean floor.

**Working.**
$$J = 0.3 \times 3 = 0.9$$

**Answer.** $\mathbf{0.9}$ m.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: The bench shown is 10 m high and requires 0.9 m of subdrill. Compute the total drilled hole depth (m).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Hole depth = bench height + subdrill.

**Working.**
$$H_d = 10 + 0.9 = 10.9$$

**Answer.** $\mathbf{10.9}$ m.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: A blasthole is charged over an 8 m explosive column with a linear loading density of 5 kg/m (as shown). Compute the charge per hole (kg).

figure.caption: Charged blasthole
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 150 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='55' y='20' width='30' height='160' fill='#e5e7eb' stroke='#475569'/><rect x='55' y='40' width='30' height='40' fill='#cbd5e1'/><text x='90' y='62' font-size='8'>stemming</text><rect x='55' y='80' width='30' height='100' fill='#f59e0b'/><text x='90' y='128' font-size='8'>charge 8 m</text><text x='90' y='143' font-size='8'>5 kg/m</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Charge = column length × linear loading density.

**Working.**
$$Q = 8 \times 5 = 40$$

**Answer.** $\mathbf{40}$ kg per hole.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: A structure is 100 m from a blast where 25 kg of explosive detonates per delay (as shown). Compute the square-root scaled distance (m/kg^0.5).

figure.caption: Blast and structure
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 130' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><circle cx='40' cy='90' r='10' fill='#dc2626'/><text x='20' y='115' font-size='8'>blast W=25 kg</text><rect x='190' y='70' width='30' height='40' fill='#94a3b8' stroke='#475569'/><text x='176' y='125' font-size='8'>structure</text><line x1='52' y1='90' x2='188' y2='90' stroke='#475569' stroke-dasharray='4 3' marker-end='url(#sd)'/><defs><marker id='sd' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0 0 L6 3 L0 6 Z' fill='#475569'/></marker></defs><text x='95' y='84' font-size='8'>R=100 m</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Scaled distance $SD = R/\sqrt{W}$.

**Working.**
$$SD = \frac{100}{\sqrt{25}} = \frac{100}{5} = 20$$

**Answer.** $\mathbf{20}$ m/kg^0.5.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: Using the USBM relation $PPV = K\,SD^{-1.6}$ with $K = 1140$ and the scaled distance of 20 (from the blast shown), compute the peak particle velocity (mm/s).

figure.caption: Blast and structure
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 130' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><circle cx='40' cy='90' r='10' fill='#dc2626'/><text x='20' y='115' font-size='8'>blast W=25 kg</text><rect x='190' y='70' width='30' height='40' fill='#94a3b8' stroke='#475569'/><text x='176' y='125' font-size='8'>structure</text><line x1='52' y1='90' x2='188' y2='90' stroke='#475569' stroke-dasharray='4 3' marker-end='url(#sd)'/><defs><marker id='sd' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0 0 L6 3 L0 6 Z' fill='#475569'/></marker></defs><text x='95' y='84' font-size='8'>R=100 m</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Apply the attenuation law $PPV = K\,SD^{-1.6}$.

**Working.**
$$PPV = 1140 \times 20^{-1.6} = \frac{1140}{20^{1.6}} = \frac{1140}{120.6} \approx 9.45$$

**Answer.** $\approx \mathbf{9.45}$ mm/s.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: To keep vibration within limits a minimum scaled distance of 25 must be maintained. For a structure 50 m away (as shown), compute the maximum allowable charge per delay (kg).

figure.caption: Charge limit by distance
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 130' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><circle cx='40' cy='90' r='10' fill='#dc2626'/><text x='22' y='115' font-size='8'>blast</text><rect x='190' y='70' width='30' height='40' fill='#94a3b8' stroke='#475569'/><text x='176' y='125' font-size='8'>structure</text><line x1='52' y1='90' x2='188' y2='90' stroke='#475569' stroke-dasharray='4 3' marker-end='url(#sd)'/><defs><marker id='sd' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0 0 L6 3 L0 6 Z' fill='#475569'/></marker></defs><text x='100' y='84' font-size='8'>R=50 m</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** From $SD = R/\sqrt{W}$, the maximum charge is $W = (R/SD)^2$.

**Working.**
$$W = \left(\frac{50}{25}\right)^2 = 2^2 = 4$$

**Answer.** $\mathbf{4}$ kg per delay.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: A row of the pattern shown is 40 m long with holes at 4 m spacing. How many holes are in the row?
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Holes in a row = length ÷ spacing + 1.

**Working.**
$$N = \frac{40}{4} + 1 = 10 + 1 = 11$$

**Answer.** $\mathbf{11}$ holes.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: The blast pattern shown has 3 rows of 11 holes each. How many blastholes are there in total?

figure.caption: Multi-row pattern
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 140' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><g fill='#1f2937'><circle cx='40' cy='40' r='4'/><circle cx='75' cy='40' r='4'/><circle cx='110' cy='40' r='4'/><circle cx='145' cy='40' r='4'/><circle cx='180' cy='40' r='4'/><circle cx='40' cy='75' r='4'/><circle cx='75' cy='75' r='4'/><circle cx='110' cy='75' r='4'/><circle cx='145' cy='75' r='4'/><circle cx='180' cy='75' r='4'/><circle cx='40' cy='110' r='4'/><circle cx='75' cy='110' r='4'/><circle cx='110' cy='110' r='4'/><circle cx='145' cy='110' r='4'/><circle cx='180' cy='110' r='4'/></g><text x='40' y='132' font-size='8'>3 rows x 11 holes</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Total holes = rows × holes per row.

**Working.**
$$N = 3 \times 11 = 33$$

**Answer.** $\mathbf{33}$ holes.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: For the 33-hole pattern shown (burden 3 m, spacing 4 m, bench height 10 m), compute the total in-situ rock volume broken (m³).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Volume = burden × spacing × height × number of holes.

**Working.**
$$V = 3 \times 4 \times 10 \times 33 = 39600$$

**Answer.** $\mathbf{39600}$ m³.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: The blast shown breaks 39600 m³ of rock of density 2.5 t/m³. Compute the tonnage produced (t).

figure.caption: Broken volume
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 120' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='30' y='35' width='140' height='60' fill='#d6d3d1' stroke='#475569'/><text x='100' y='70' font-size='9' text-anchor='middle'>39600 m^3</text><text x='40' y='110' font-size='8'>density 2.5 t/m^3</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Tonnage = volume × density.

**Working.**
$$T = 39600 \times 2.5 = 99000$$

**Answer.** $\mathbf{99000}$ t.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: The holes in the row shown are fired with the millisecond delays marked. The main reason for delay blasting is to:

figure.caption: Delay sequence
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 110' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><g fill='#1f2937'><circle cx='40' cy='50' r='5'/><circle cx='90' cy='50' r='5'/><circle cx='140' cy='50' r='5'/><circle cx='190' cy='50' r='5'/></g><g font-size='8' fill='#dc2626'><text x='34' y='75'>0</text><text x='80' y='75'>25</text><text x='128' y='75'>50</text><text x='176' y='75'>75 ms</text></g></svg>

options: provide relief and improve fragmentation while lowering ground vibration
increase the total charge per delay
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Sequenced delays create free faces and split the charge in time.

**Reasoning.** Each delayed row breaks toward the void left by the previous one, improving fragmentation and muckpile shape, while firing less explosive per instant reduces the peak charge per delay and hence ground vibration.

**Answer.** Provide relief and improve fragmentation while lowering ground vibration.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: The drill-round cut shown uses angled holes that converge to a wedge at the centre of the face. This is a:

figure.caption: Face cut
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 200 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='20' y='20' width='160' height='120' fill='#e5e7eb' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='100' y1='80' x2='50' y2='30' stroke='#1f2937' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='100' y1='80' x2='150' y2='30' stroke='#1f2937' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='100' y1='80' x2='50' y2='130' stroke='#1f2937' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='100' y1='80' x2='150' y2='130' stroke='#1f2937' stroke-width='2'/><text x='52' y='152' font-size='8'>angled holes meet at centre</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** A wedge cut uses inclined holes meeting in a V to open a free face.

**Reasoning.** Angled holes converging to a central wedge prise out a triangular wedge of rock, creating the initial cavity; this is the classic wedge (V) cut, distinct from the parallel-hole burn cut.

**Answer.** Wedge (V) cut.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: The cut shown uses parallel holes drilled perpendicular to the face, with one or more uncharged large-diameter relief holes at the centre. This is a:

figure.caption: Parallel-hole cut
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 200 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='20' y='20' width='160' height='120' fill='#e5e7eb' stroke='#475569'/><g fill='#1f2937'><circle cx='80' cy='70' r='4'/><circle cx='110' cy='70' r='4'/><circle cx='80' cy='100' r='4'/><circle cx='110' cy='100' r='4'/></g><circle cx='95' cy='85' r='8' fill='none' stroke='#475569'/><text x='40' y='152' font-size='8'>parallel holes + uncharged reliever</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** A burn cut uses parallel holes around empty relief holes to create a cavity normal to the face.

**Reasoning.** Charged parallel holes break toward the uncharged large-diameter relief hole(s), producing a clean burn cavity; this parallel-hole geometry defines the burn cut, allowing deeper, straight pulls.

**Answer.** Burn cut.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: For the presplit line shown, hole spacing is taken as 12 times the hole diameter. With a hole diameter of 0.05 m, compute the presplit hole spacing (m).

figure.caption: Presplit line
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 120' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><g fill='#1f2937'><circle cx='40' cy='50' r='3'/><circle cx='60' cy='50' r='3'/><circle cx='80' cy='50' r='3'/><circle cx='100' cy='50' r='3'/><circle cx='120' cy='50' r='3'/><circle cx='140' cy='50' r='3'/><circle cx='160' cy='50' r='3'/><circle cx='180' cy='50' r='3'/></g><line x1='40' y1='65' x2='180' y2='65' stroke='#dc2626'/><text x='50' y='90' font-size='8'>presplit line, d=0.05 m</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Presplit spacing is a small multiple of hole diameter (here 12 d).

**Working.**
$$S = 12 \times 0.05 = 0.6$$

**Answer.** $\mathbf{0.6}$ m.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: The detonation front travels along the explosive column as shown. The speed of this front is the explosive's:

figure.caption: Detonation along column
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 110' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='30' y='45' width='180' height='20' fill='#f59e0b' stroke='#92400e'/><circle cx='40' cy='55' r='8' fill='#dc2626'/><line x1='50' y1='55' x2='120' y2='55' stroke='#fff' stroke-dasharray='4 3' marker-end='url(#vd)'/><defs><marker id='vd' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0 0 L6 3 L0 6 Z' fill='#fff'/></marker></defs><text x='60' y='90' font-size='8'>detonation front along column</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** The detonation wave speed is the velocity of detonation.

**Reasoning.** The rate at which the reaction front propagates along the charge is the velocity of detonation (VOD), a key measure of an explosive's shattering (brisance) power.

**Answer.** Velocity of detonation (VOD).

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: If the muckpile shown is too coarse, the most direct design change to produce finer fragmentation is to:

figure.caption: Muckpile fragments
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 130' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><g fill='#a16207' stroke='#92400e'><polygon points='30,90 55,80 70,100 45,110'/><polygon points='80,85 110,75 120,100 90,108'/><polygon points='130,90 150,82 162,102 138,110'/><polygon points='170,88 195,80 200,104 175,108'/></g><text x='40' y='125' font-size='8'>muckpile fragment sizes</text></svg>

options: increase the powder factor (tighter pattern / more charge)
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Fragmentation gets finer as energy per unit volume (powder factor) rises.

**Reasoning.** A tighter pattern or higher charge raises the powder factor, putting more energy into each cubic metre of rock and reducing mean fragment size. Increasing burden/spacing would coarsen the muck.

**Answer.** Increase the powder factor (tighter pattern / more charge).

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: The rock fragments thrown well beyond the muckpile in the figure are flyrock. The most common cause is:

figure.caption: Flyrock from a blast
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 130' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='20' y='80' width='90' height='40' fill='#fde9c8' stroke='#92400e'/><circle cx='40' cy='90' r='8' fill='#dc2626'/><g fill='#475569'><circle cx='150' cy='40' r='3'/><circle cx='180' cy='55' r='3'/><circle cx='200' cy='35' r='3'/></g><path d='M48 84 Q120 10 200 35' fill='none' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-dasharray='3 2'/><text x='110' y='120' font-size='8'>ejected fragments (flyrock)</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Flyrock results when gas energy escapes uncontrolled through a weak zone.

**Reasoning.** Inadequate burden or short/poor stemming lets high-pressure gases vent violently, ejecting fragments far beyond the muckpile. Adequate burden, stemming and proper loading control flyrock.

**Answer.** Insufficient burden or stemming.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: A blasthole is advanced $12\ \text{m}$ in $4\ \text{min}$ (figure). Compute the average penetration rate (m/min).

figure.caption: Drilling penetration
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 160 170' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='60' y='20' width='16' height='40' fill='#94a3b8' stroke='#475569'/><rect x='64' y='60' width='8' height='80' fill='#6b7280'/><rect x='30' y='140' width='100' height='20' fill='#a8a29e' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='100' y1='60' x2='100' y2='140' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#dr)'/><defs><marker id='dr' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='4' refY='6' orient='auto'><path d='M0 0 L3 6 L6 0 Z' fill='#dc2626'/></marker></defs><text x='14' y='130' font-size='8'>12 m in 4 min</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Penetration rate $=\dfrac{\text{depth}}{\text{time}}$.

**Working.**
$$r=\frac{12}{4}=3\ \text{m/min}$$

**Answer.** $3$ m/min.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: Using the rule of thumb $B\approx25\,d$ (with $d$ the hole diameter), compute the burden for a $0.1\ \text{m}$ diameter blasthole (m).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Burden from the diameter rule, $B=25\,d$.

**Working.**
$$B=25\times0.1=2.5\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** $2.5$ m.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: For a staggered pattern with spacing-to-burden ratio $1.25$ and burden $B=3\ \text{m}$, compute the spacing (m).

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: A blast uses $200\ \text{kg}$ of explosive to break $1000\ \mathrm{m^3}$ of rock (figure). Compute the powder factor ($\mathrm{kg/m^3}$).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Powder factor $=\dfrac{\text{explosive mass}}{\text{rock volume}}$.

**Working.**
$$PF=\frac{200}{1000}=0.2\ \mathrm{kg/m^3}$$

**Answer.** $0.2\ \mathrm{kg/m^3}$.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: A blasthole has a charge column of $6\ \text{m}$ with a linear charge density of $8\ \text{kg/m}$ (figure). Compute the explosive charge per hole (kg).

figure.caption: Charged blasthole
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 130 180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='50' y='20' width='24' height='150' fill='#e5e7eb' stroke='#475569'/><rect x='52' y='30' width='20' height='40' fill='#cbd5e1'/><rect x='52' y='70' width='20' height='90' fill='#f59e0b'/><text x='80' y='55' font-size='8'>stemming</text><text x='80' y='120' font-size='8'>charge 6 m</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Charge $=$ column length $\times$ linear density.

**Working.**
$$W=6\times8=48\ \text{kg}$$

**Answer.** $48$ kg.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: A bench blasthole has burden $B=3\ \text{m}$, spacing $S=4\ \text{m}$ and bench height $H=10\ \text{m}$ (figure). Compute the rock volume broken per hole ($\mathrm{m^3}$).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Volume per hole $=B\times S\times H$.

**Working.**
$$V=3\times4\times10=120\ \mathrm{m^3}$$

**Answer.** $120\ \mathrm{m^3}$.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: A blast block plan of area $600\ \mathrm{m^2}$ is drilled on a pattern with $B\times S=12\ \mathrm{m^2}$ per hole (figure). Compute the number of holes.

figure.caption: Drill pattern plan
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 200 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><g fill='#475569'><circle cx='40' cy='40' r='4'/><circle cx='90' cy='40' r='4'/><circle cx='140' cy='40' r='4'/><circle cx='40' cy='90' r='4'/><circle cx='90' cy='90' r='4'/><circle cx='140' cy='90' r='4'/></g><line x1='40' y1='115' x2='90' y2='115' stroke='#2563eb' marker-end='url(#p)' marker-start='url(#p)'/><line x1='165' y1='40' x2='165' y2='90' stroke='#16a34a' marker-end='url(#p)' marker-start='url(#p)'/><defs><marker id='p' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='3' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0 0 L6 3 L0 6 Z' fill='#475569'/></marker></defs><text x='58' y='128' font-size='8'>S</text><text x='170' y='70' font-size='8'>B</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Number of holes $=\dfrac{\text{plan area}}{B\times S}$.

**Working.**
$$N=\frac{600}{12}=50$$

**Answer.** $50$ holes.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: A blast round of $20$ holes each carries $40\ \text{kg}$ of explosive (figure). Compute the total explosive used (kg).

figure.caption: Round of charged holes
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 200 130' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><g fill='#f59e0b' stroke='#b45309'><rect x='30' y='40' width='10' height='60'/><rect x='60' y='40' width='10' height='60'/><rect x='90' y='40' width='10' height='60'/><rect x='120' y='40' width='10' height='60'/><rect x='150' y='40' width='10' height='60'/></g><text x='30' y='120' font-size='8'>20 holes x 40 kg each</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Total charge $=$ holes $\times$ charge per hole.

**Working.**
$$W=20\times40=800\ \text{kg}$$

**Answer.** $800$ kg.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: A detonation front travels $6\ \text{m}$ along a charge in $1\ \text{ms}$ (figure). Compute the velocity of detonation (m/s).

figure.caption: Velocity of detonation
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 100' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='20' y='45' width='180' height='16' fill='#f59e0b' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='20' y1='53' x2='180' y2='53' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#vo)'/><defs><marker id='vo' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0 0 L6 3 L0 6 Z' fill='#dc2626'/></marker></defs><text x='40' y='85' font-size='8'>detonation: 6 m in 1 ms</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** $\text{VOD}=\dfrac{\text{distance}}{\text{time}}$.

**Working.**
$$\text{VOD}=\frac{6}{1\times10^{-3}}=6000\ \text{m/s}$$

**Answer.** $6000$ m/s.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: Using the rule $T\approx20\,d$, compute the stemming length for a $0.1\ \text{m}$ diameter blasthole (m).

figure.caption: Stemming length
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 130 180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='50' y='20' width='24' height='150' fill='#e5e7eb' stroke='#475569'/><rect x='52' y='22' width='20' height='40' fill='#cbd5e1'/><rect x='52' y='62' width='20' height='98' fill='#f59e0b'/><line x1='80' y1='22' x2='80' y2='62' stroke='#2563eb' marker-end='url(#sm)' marker-start='url(#sm)'/><defs><marker id='sm' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='3' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0 0 L6 3 L0 6 Z' fill='#2563eb'/></marker></defs><text x='86' y='45' font-size='8'>stemming</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Stemming length $T=20\,d$.

**Working.**
$$T=20\times0.1=2\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** $2$ m.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: A blast drills $100\ \text{m}$ of hole to break $1200\ \mathrm{m^3}$ of rock (figure). Compute the specific drilling ($\mathrm{m/m^3}$, three decimals).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Specific drilling $=\dfrac{\text{metres drilled}}{\text{volume broken}}$.

**Working.**
$$\frac{100}{1200}=0.083\ \mathrm{m/m^3}$$

**Answer.** $0.083\ \mathrm{m/m^3}$.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: Using the rule $J=0.3\,B$, compute the subdrill (drilled below grade) for a burden $B=3\ \text{m}$ (m).

figure.caption: Subdrill below the bench floor
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 200 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><polygon points='20,40 120,40 120,120 20,120' fill='#d6d3d1' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='70' y1='30' x2='70' y2='140' stroke='#6b7280' stroke-width='3'/><line x1='20' y1='120' x2='180' y2='120' stroke='#475569' stroke-dasharray='4 2'/><text x='75' y='150' font-size='8'>subdrill below grade</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Subdrill $J=0.3\,B$.

**Working.**
$$J=0.3\times3=0.9\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** $0.9$ m.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: A cylindrical explosive column of diameter $0.1\ \text{m}$ and length $6\ \text{m}$ has a density of $900\ \mathrm{kg/m^3}$ (figure). Compute the charge mass (kg, two decimals).

figure.caption: Cylindrical charge
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 130 180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='52' y='30' width='22' height='120' rx='4' fill='#f59e0b' stroke='#b45309'/><line x1='52' y1='160' x2='74' y2='160' stroke='#2563eb' marker-end='url(#cd)' marker-start='url(#cd)'/><defs><marker id='cd' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='3' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0 0 L6 3 L0 6 Z' fill='#2563eb'/></marker></defs><text x='80' y='95' font-size='8'>d=0.1 m</text><text x='8' y='100' font-size='8'>L=6 m</text></svg>

questions.solution:
