**Concept.** $W=\dfrac{\pi}{4}d^2 L\,\rho$.

**Working.**
$$W=\frac{\pi}{4}(0.1)^2(6)(900)=0.7854\times0.01\times6\times900=42.41\ \text{kg}$$

**Answer.** $42.41$ kg.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: The numbers in the firing pattern shown are millisecond delays between holes. Such delay initiation is used mainly to:

figure.caption: Delay sequence between holes
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 120' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><g fill='#475569'><circle cx='40' cy='60' r='4'/><circle cx='90' cy='60' r='4'/><circle cx='140' cy='60' r='4'/><circle cx='190' cy='60' r='4'/></g><text x='32' y='85' font-size='8'>0</text><text x='80' y='85' font-size='8'>25</text><text x='126' y='85' font-size='8'>50</text><text x='176' y='85' font-size='8'>75 ms</text><text x='40' y='40' font-size='8'>delay sequence</text></svg>

options: improve fragmentation and reduce ground vibration by sequencing the holes
make all holes detonate at exactly the same instant
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Delay blasting controls the timing of individual holes.

**Reasoning.** Firing holes in sequence provides relief for later holes, improving fragmentation and muckpile shape while spreading the charge weight per delay, which lowers peak ground vibration.

**Answer.** Improve fragmentation and reduce ground vibration by sequencing the holes.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: A bench blast uses $200\ \text{kg}$ of explosive to break $500\ \mathrm{m^3}$ of rock (figure). Compute the powder factor ($\mathrm{kg/m^3}$).

figure.caption: Bench blast block
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 140' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='40' y='30' width='140' height='80' fill='#d6d3d1' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='70' y1='30' x2='70' y2='110' stroke='#475569' stroke-dasharray='3 3'/><text x='52' y='128' font-size='8'>200 kg breaks 500 m^3</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Powder factor $=\dfrac{\text{explosive mass}}{\text{rock volume}}$.

**Working.**
$$PF=\frac{200}{500}=0.4\ \mathrm{kg/m^3}$$

**Answer.** $0.4\ \mathrm{kg/m^3}$.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: A blasthole has a charged column length of $6\ \text{m}$ and a linear charge density of $8\ \mathrm{kg/m}$ (figure). Compute the explosive charge per hole (kg).

figure.caption: Charged column
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 120 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='50' y='20' width='20' height='120' fill='#e5e7eb' stroke='#475569'/><rect x='50' y='70' width='20' height='70' fill='#fca5a5' stroke='#475569'/><text x='8' y='150' font-size='8'>column 6 m, 8 kg/m</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Charge $=$ column length $\times$ linear charge density.

**Working.**
$$Q=6\times8=48\ \text{kg}$$

**Answer.** $48$ kg.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: A structure is $100\ \text{m}$ from a blast that detonates a maximum charge of $25\ \text{kg}$ per delay (figure). Compute the square-root scaled distance ($\mathrm{m/kg^{1/2}}$).

figure.caption: Distance from blast
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 110' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><circle cx='30' cy='70' r='6' fill='#dc2626'/><rect x='190' y='45' width='30' height='30' fill='#94a3b8' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='36' y1='70' x2='190' y2='62' stroke='#475569' stroke-dasharray='4 3'/><text x='60' y='55' font-size='8'>D=100 m</text><text x='14' y='95' font-size='8'>W=25 kg/delay</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Scaled distance $SD=\dfrac{D}{\sqrt{W}}$.

**Working.**
$$SD=\frac{100}{\sqrt{25}}=\frac{100}{5}=20\ \mathrm{m/kg^{1/2}}$$

**Answer.** $20\ \mathrm{m/kg^{1/2}}$.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: A production hole has burden $3\ \text{m}$, spacing $3.5\ \text{m}$ and bench height $10\ \text{m}$ (figure). Compute the rock volume broken per hole ($\mathrm{m^3}$).

figure.caption: Bench geometry per hole
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='40' y='20' width='140' height='100' fill='#d6d3d1' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='110' y1='20' x2='110' y2='120' stroke='#475569' stroke-dasharray='3 3'/><text x='44' y='140' font-size='8'>B=3, S=3.5, H=10 m</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Volume per hole $=B\times S\times H$.

**Working.**
$$V=3\times3.5\times10=105\ \mathrm{m^3}$$

**Answer.** $105\ \mathrm{m^3}$.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: For a bench blast the burden is $3\ \text{m}$ and the subdrilling is taken as $0.3\,B$ (figure). Compute the subdrill length (m).

figure.caption: Subdrill below grade
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 200 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='40' y='20' width='120' height='90' fill='#d6d3d1' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='40' y1='110' x2='160' y2='110' stroke='#1f2937'/><rect x='95' y='110' width='10' height='25' fill='#fca5a5' stroke='#475569'/><text x='44' y='150' font-size='8'>subdrill = 0.3 x B, B=3 m</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Subdrill $=0.3\,B$.

**Working.**
$$J=0.3\times3=0.9\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** $0.9$ m.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: The figure shows a row of holes fired with increasing inter-hole delay along the row. The main purpose of using delays between holes is to:

figure.caption: Delayed initiation sequence
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 90' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><g fill='#dc2626'><circle cx='30' cy='50' r='6'/><circle cx='80' cy='50' r='6'/><circle cx='130' cy='50' r='6'/><circle cx='180' cy='50' r='6'/></g><text x='20' y='30' font-size='8'>0 ms</text><text x='68' y='30' font-size='8'>25 ms</text><text x='118' y='30' font-size='8'>50 ms</text><text x='168' y='30' font-size='8'>75 ms</text></svg>

options: provide free faces for later holes and reduce ground vibration
increase the total charge detonating per instant
eliminate the burden ahead of each hole
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Delay blasting.

**Reasoning.** Firing holes in sequence lets earlier holes create a new free face for later holes, improving fragmentation, and it limits the maximum charge per delay, which reduces peak ground vibration.

**Answer.** Provide free faces for later holes and reduce ground vibration.
